content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance •• Administration •• Executive/Legal •• Enforcement
(916) 322-5662       322-5660        322-5901        322-6441

August 24, 1984


Elizabeth Dahl
Administrative Assistant
Senator Ellis' Office
State Capitol, Room 4053
Sacramento, CA 95814

                                                    SUPERSEDED
                                                    by: 96-154


                                    Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                         Our File No. A-84-212

Dear Ms. Dahl:

     This letter is sent in response to your request for advice
dated August 20, 1984, and our telephone conversations of
August 20 and August 23.

     My understanding of the situation is as follows.  You are
planning a retirement party for an administrative assistant to a
state legislator.  The party will include hors d'oeuvres and
cocktails, and you anticipate at this time that the cost of the
event will exceed $10 for each person attending.  While
lobbyists will not be participating or assisting in the
planning, coordinating or any other arrangements for the party,
you want to request from lobbyists a $50 donation to assist in
the funding of the party.  Since several legislative officials
(including yourself) will be attending the party, you asked what
the reporting requirements would be for the legislative
officials and whether the lobbyist gift prohibition in
Government Code Section 86203 would apply.1/

     1.  Reporting by Legislative Officials.

     All legislative officials are required to file statements of
economic interests disclosing their investments, interests in
real property, and income.  Sections 87200, et seg.  Income
includes all gifts (Sections 82028 and 82030), and gifts of $50
or more must be reported (Section 87207(a)(1)).  The Commission
has previously determined that the value of a gift such as the
party you propose here is the value of the benefits the official
personally receives by attending the event.  Gutierrez Opinion,
3 FPPC Opinions 44 (No. 76-081, June 7, 1977( (copy enclosed).

_________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code.

Elizabeth Dahl
August 24, 1984
Page 2


In the Gutierrez Opinion, the Commission also stated that the
proper way to establish this value is to divide the actual cost
of sponsoring the event by the number of persons who attended.
Accordingly, so long as the value of the event to each official
is calculated by this formula and remains under $50, the officials
will not incur any reporting obligations by attending this
event.

     2.  Lobbyist Gift Prohibition.

     As noted above, you intend to solicit lobbyists for a $50
donation to help pay for the party.

     Section 86203 prohibits lobbyists from making gifts directly
or indirectly to, among others, a legislative official
aggregating more than $10 in a calendar month, or to arrange for
the making of any such gift by any other person.  This
prohibition means that a lobbyist may not make any payment of
more than $10 which results in a gift aggregating more than $10
in value to a legislative official nor may a lobbyist assist in
the arrangements for a gift of more than $10 to a legislative
official.  If your estimate is correct that the value of the
party to the attendees will exceed $10, then lobbyists may not
make a payment of more than $10 towards the party.  If a
lobbyist is going to attend the party, he or she may pay for the
value of what he or she receives (e.g., if the party is going to
cost approximately $15 person, a lobbyist may pay $15), and,
in addition, the lobbyist may make a donation of $10 or less.

     However, if the value of the party to the attendees remains
below $10 per person, the lobbyists may make donations of any
amount for the party.  These donations would be reportable by
the lobbyists on their lobbyist statements, but they would not
be prohibited so long as no legislative official receives
benefits in connection with the party aggregating over $10 in
value.

     I trust that this letter answers your concerns.  Please feel
free to contact me if you have further questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Staff Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosure

August 20, 1984


Diane Fishburn
F.P.P.C.
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Diane:

I am requesting a written opinion from you in reference
to a retirement party I am planning to have for an
Administrative Assistant.

I will be calling upon lobbyists for a $50.00 donation
to assist in the funding for this party.

Your prompt response will be appreciated.

Thank you.

Elizabeth Dahl

ELIZABETH DAHL, A.A.
Room 4053
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA, 95814

PHONE: 445-3952

sections.question: What are the reporting requirements for legislative officials attending a retirement party where lobbyists are being solicited for $50 donations, and does the lobbyist gift prohibition in Government Code Section 86203 apply?
sections.conclusion: Legislative officials attending the party need only report gifts of $50 or more. The value of the gift is calculated by dividing total party costs by the number of attendees; if this per-person value stays below $50, officials have no reporting obligations. Regarding lobbyist donations: if the per-person value exceeds $10, lobbyists may not donate more than $10 total and cannot pay more than $10 for their own attendance without violating §86203. If per-person value is below $10, lobbyists may donate any amount and attend freely.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester is planning a retirement party for a legislative administrative assistant and soliciting $50 donations from lobbyists. The legal questions concern: (1) what reporting obligations legislative officials have for attending this party, and (2) whether the lobbyist gift prohibition in Gov. Code §86203 applies to these donations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Legislative officials attending must report the gift only if their per-person share (calculated as total cost divided by attendees) exceeds $50. Lobbyists are prohibited from donating more than $10 per calendar month to legislative officials, so their donations to this party are limited to $10 unless the per-person party value stays below $10, in which case unlimited donations are permitted.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document marked 'SUPERSEDED' at top. Clear question-and-answer structure with two main legal issues. Both incoming request letter and FPPC response included in document. Minor formatting variation in statutory citations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the reporting requirements for legislative officials attending a retirement party where lobbyists are being solicited for $50 donations, and does the lobbyist gift prohibition in Government Code Section 86203 apply?

CONCLUSION: Legislative officials attending the party need only report gifts of $50 or more. The value of the gift is calculated by dividing total party costs by the number of attendees; if this per-person value stays below $50, officials have no reporting obligations. Regarding lobbyist donations: if the per-person value exceeds $10, lobbyists may not donate more than $10 total and cannot pay more than $10 for their own attendance without violating §86203. If per-person value is below $10, lobbyists may donate any amount and attend freely.

embedding.summary: FPPC Staff Counsel Diane Fishburn responds to Elizabeth Dahl's request regarding reporting requirements and lobbyist gift restrictions for a planned administrative assistant retirement party funded partially through $50 lobbyist solicitations. The letter clarifies the calculation method for gift valuation and establishes specific limitations on lobbyist contributions based on per-person event costs.
